author: 0xrubo
content: <@774033563732541451> is `eip8025_optional_proofs` the only diffence from bal tests?

author: jsign
content: In regards to new vs existing tests, yes. But note that in all existing tests (i.e. not only `eip8025_optional_proofs`), we still generate the `executionWitness`. So it is actually meaningful to run all tests filled in the release to check `executionWitness` correctness (and also `stateless{Input/Output}` if you want to test guest programs).

author: jsign
content:
So the way to think about it is:
- All tests have the `executionWitness` to check, so makes sense to run all tests.
- `eip8025_optional_proofs` have specialized tests that test more detailed angles about execution witness border cases (e.g. that might not appear by pure chance in all the rest of >10k tests that already exist)

author: 0xrubo
content: for exec clients, i believe eip8025 doesn't make sense, right? it's consensus only

author: jsign
content: Nono, it makes sense for ELs. The `eip8025_optional_proofs` refers to tests for the execution witness generation angle of the EIP. Since execution witness is part of eip8025

author: jsign
content: In the same way that the `statelessInput` and `statelessOutput` fixture fields are the inputs and outputs of the guest program. i.e. this is related to guest program testing, not stateful EL execution witness generation testing.

author: 0xrubo
content: that's interesting. we run those fixtures in automated way, but for the guest program, i think we need a different approach. let me check those fields. where exactly are they?

author: jsign
content: For example, many `eip8025_optional_proofs` tests (i.e. `test_witness_validation_*` files) are test that try to send an invalid execution witness which the guest program must reject -- so those are more intented to guest program correctness in execution witness _validation_.

author: jsign
content:
In the execution specs, we also have the specs for the guest program. This defines the raw input bytes and output bytes for them. https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/blob/713d1eed0d3b730b7d3c72c961fc4d7ed0b8d656/src/ethereum/forks/amsterdam/stateless_guest.py#L33-L41
So those fiels are exactly the input and output for the fixtures, so you can also run all tests with the guest program.
